1.blurb: The world's first cities emerged in southern Mesopotamia around 3500 BCE, developing the cuneiform writing, temple economies, and urban culture that would eventually reach Anatolia through Assyrian trade networks. The Hittites inherited Mesopotamian writing and diplomatic conventions wholesale.

The Old Assyrian merchants who established the karum trade colonies across Anatolia around 1950 BCE were based at Assur on the Tigris. Their 23,000 cuneiform tablets from Kanesh are one of the richest archives of ancient commercial activity ever found.

2.blurb: Hammurabi of Babylon conquered most of Mesopotamia around 1792-1750 BCE and is famous for his law code. His dynasty was the one Mursili I destroyed when the Hittites sacked Babylon in 1595 BCE, ending the First Babylonian Dynasty and leaving a power vacuum the Kassites filled.

While the Hittite Old Kingdom was struggling through palace assassinations, China's Shang dynasty was at its height, working bronze and developing oracle bone script. The two civilizations developed in total isolation from each other, on opposite ends of the Eurasian landmass.

3.blurb: Egypt's pharaonic tradition, stretching back to the unification around 3100 BCE, produced a divine kingship so absolute that the idea of importing a foreign prince to become pharaoh was unthinkable. The Zannanza affair's extraordinary nature only makes sense against that three-thousand-year tradition.

The Mitanni kingdom that Suppiluliuma destroyed had also dominated Assyria for over a century. When the Hittites broke Mitanni, they inadvertently freed Assyria to begin its own rise to power -- Ashur-uballit I exploited the vacuum to establish Assyrian independence.

4.blurb: The Mesopotamian mythology that the Hittites absorbed included creation stories, divine succession narratives, and ritual traditions stretching back to the Sumerians. The Hurro-Hittite Song of Kumarbi drew on these traditions and transmitted them westward toward the Aegean, where they influenced Greek mythology.

5.blurb: The diplomatic tradition of vassal treaties, originating in Mesopotamia, was perfected by the Hittites into a sophisticated system of mutual obligations enforced by divine witnesses. The Egyptian-Hittite Peace Treaty was the culmination of this tradition -- a bilateral pact between equals that anticipated modern international law by three millennia.

6.blurb: Nubian goods on the Uluburun ship demonstrate that the Late Bronze Age trade network reached deep into Africa. Nubia was a major supplier of gold, ebony, and exotic materials to Egypt, and through Egypt to the broader Mediterranean trading system the Hittites participated in.

7.blurb: The Elamite bureaucratic tradition, based at Susa in southwestern Iran, survived the fall of Elam itself and became the administrative spine of the later Persian Empire. The Hittites lacked this kind of deep institutional infrastructure, which helps explain why their empire collapsed so completely when the top fell.

The Bronze Age Collapse around 1200-1150 BCE destroyed or severely damaged nearly every major civilization in the eastern Mediterranean. Mesopotamia survived but was badly weakened -- Assyria shrank to a rump state and took two centuries to recover, while the Kassite dynasty in Babylon was overthrown.

Mycenaean Greece, which had absorbed the earlier Minoan civilization of Crete, was among the casualties of the Bronze Age Collapse. The palace systems at Mycenae, Pylos, and Tiryns were destroyed, and Greece entered a dark age that lasted four centuries -- a parallel catastrophe to the Hittite collapse.

Assyria was one of the few states to survive the Bronze Age Collapse, though barely. Reduced to a rump state centered on Assur, it took two centuries to recover -- but when it did, the Neo-Assyrian Empire would become the most powerful military force the ancient world had yet seen.

8.blurb: Sargon II conquered Carchemish in 717 BCE, ending the last major Neo-Hittite state and absorbing five centuries of Hittite cultural continuity into the Assyrian provincial system. The Neo-Assyrian Empire systematically dismantled every remaining Neo-Hittite kingdom by 700 BCE.

The Persian Empire that Cyrus the Great built adopted the Hittite model of governing through treaties and managed integration rather than pure military force. The Hittite vassal treaty system, the inclusion of foreign gods, and the emphasis on diplomacy over destruction all anticipated Persian imperial practice.

The massive winged bulls and carved relief panels of the Assyrian palaces at Nimrud drew on artistic traditions that ran through the Neo-Hittite portal sculptures back to the lions and sphinxes of Hattusa itself -- a chain of artistic influence spanning five centuries.
